sentences.text: Well, you know, like I-- like I said, that we used to get some weekly potatoes or some, some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call-- coal or wood to-- to burn, or whatever.
We get some help.
Maybe sometimes some flour, you know, from, from Polacks, from our farmers.
I didn't-- I didn't mean that they give it for nothing, you know.
We used to-- like I say, we had textiles, and my mother had some money, and we used to buy from them.
But it was still good that we could get from them, because everything, you know, was-- it was rationed and you couldn't get anything unless-- you know, whatever you got.
So they were not allowed to sell it exactly.
So [INAUDIBLE] it was help for us.
They helped us.
I wouldn't say that everybody got help [? us? ] and everybody did help every Polack.
But some of them did.
A year and a half.
A year and a half.
And then where did you go?
I went to Rabka, which is another resort north of, of Nowy Targ.
Zakopane used to be south of, of, of Nowy Targ, and this is north of Nowy Targ.
No, no, no.
No.
We didn't go there.
We were sent by the Judenrat after Zakopane to go to Rabka.
Rabka is a-- like I said, a resort town.
I think it was for people which, which they suffered from, from diabetes or something.
They had some, some kind of minerals or something-- Rabka, before the war.
Right.
Rabka had a school.
It used to be a, a Polish school for, for girls.
And they took it over, and they-- and they formed-- they called it [GERMAN].
It was a school strictly for Gestapo.

In other words, they came-- they brought all the Gestapo from different parts of the country-- I don't know from what cities-- and they-- and they stayed over there, let's say six weeks or eight weeks, and they-- and they had some, some instruction.
They were instructing them, you know?
And then there came a bunch of new ones, and that's how it-- how it goes round and round.
Every six weeks or eight weeks came a different part-- different bunch of, of, of these Gestapo people.
And they were attending the school.
Were all of them German?
Yeah.
All of them were German.
Well, I would say maybe about 60.
Yes.
Between six or eight weeks.
No.
They were just-- we built a target range.
And they were-- they were-- they took them out to, you know, for target practices.
And inside, what they-- what they teach them inside, we never knew about it.
We built a-- the target range, and--
We built a target range, and we did all maintenance around the school.
Yeah.
The name was Wilhelm Rosenbaum.
He was a [GERMAN] and, as a matter of fact, I went to his trial in Germany in, in 1967-- or '68, I think it was.
I had all the papers from Germany, which-- from the trial, yes.
I was as a witness.
Yes.
Well, I was a witness from Rabka.
We, we came into Rabka from about-- in 1941.
Not only from Nowy Targ.
From Nowy Targ were on the list a few people, about maybe eight people from Nowy Targ.
The rest of them were from different part of the-- of, of that-- of our neighborhood, from little, small towns-- from Nowy Sacz, from, from Stary Sacz, from Szczawnica, which is around there.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we came to Rabka, we were still-- our, our hometown was still not evacuated.
In other words, my mother and my, my sister and my, my grandfather were still living in Nowy Targ.
We were just sent to Rabka.
I was 17.
I came to Rabka, and, and we worked.
And we worked very hard.
And they were known in Rabka, if you don't work, if they catch you, they see you not working, you get killed, which, which did happen quite a few times.
We were working in, in forest, and, and all of a sudden, you know, some, some, some people stopped for a minute, not-- stopped working.
And all of a sudden from behind the, the trees came a-- came a Gestapo.
And he killed him right on the spot.
It happened quite often.
I didn't see it, but I knew about it.
But it happened in 1942.
I never came back to Poland when there, there, there was the big selection in Poland.
In other words, the, the last day of, of, of the-- of the Jews from Nowy Targ.
It was September 1st, [BACKGROUND NOISE] 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never seen my, my mother and, and sisters again.
We were in Rabka, and that day in, in Nowy Targ, they, they took all the Jews on the [GERMAN] they called it, on the-- on the-- on the-- not too far from the rail station.
They took all the Jews then, and they took all the Judenrat and all, all these people which they couldn't work.
They took them all on the trucks-- or I don't know-- on the trucks or they walked them.
They took them to the cemetery and they killed them.
They dig the graves, and they were all killed in the same day.
The rest of the people they put on the trains, and they shipped them to Belzec.
It was in 1942.
Now--
That what?
From Belzec--
Yes.
What happened with liquidation of the town, some people survived, that, that particularly last day.
They were shipped to, to-- from that particular-- [INAUDIBLE] from that day they were shipped to different camps, young people, and they, they, they knew what happened on the Appellplatz.

They, they were not in the cemetery, but on that cemetery, that particularly day, they took, like I said, the, the Jewish Judenrat and some other Jewish people, which they, I guess, they couldn't-- they couldn't ship 'em out, or they didn't wanna ship 'em out.
They wanna kill 'em right on the spot.
There was a man named Grazgreen.
He was the father of my good friend.
And he [BACKGROUND NOISE] used to have a farm not too far from the-- from the cemetery, and he knew the, the, you know, the territory.
And he jumped over the fence.
They were all, all undressing on the-- on the cemetery.
All the Jews were undressing to be killed.
And he jumped a fence, and he ran away from it-- from the cemetery.
He was the only one to run away.
And he ran to Czechoslovakia, and he survived the war.
He knew some, some farmers over there.
He survived the war in Czechoslovakia.
And he came back to Poland in 1945 to Nowy Targ, after the Germans, you know, were defeated, after the Russian came into Poland.
And in 1945-- the end of '45 or the beginning of '46, I'm not sure-- the Polish underground killed him in Poland.
The AK.
Because he was Jew.
Because he came back to [INAUDIBLE] Poland.
As a matter of fact, I came to Nowy Targ in 1945.
And we wanna see if anybody survived.
We were so stupid in thinking that somebody survived.
Like I just said before, my father-- on the way to Wilno, I thought maybe my father survived.
We came to look for, for, for family, for survivors.
And we were only a few young people which survived from the hometown.
And I, I have seen that nobody survived, and I went back to Germany.
But a month later, three of my good friends, which survived the camps, were killed in Nowy Targ by the AK.
Yeah.
The name was-- the name-- I, I have a book.
As a matter of fact, I can show you.
I have a book which was written about the city of Nowy Targ.
It's in English and Hebrew and, and Jewish.
And you even have pictures, documented pictures from the funerals which they-- which they-- where they were buried in, in Krakow.
The name was Lonek Lindenberger.
The name of the other man-- the other friend of mine-- was Ludwig Hertz.
And one was Henik Turner.
They were all killed on the way-- they, they, they were on a truck going to, to Slovakia.
They were stopped on the road, and they were killed.
Armia Krajowa.
They were known in our neighborhood, Armia Krajowa, even during the war.
Some Jewish people were hiding in the forest and the hills.
They were sniffed out by the Armia Krajowa.
And they were killed by Armia Krajowa.
Either it, it must have been in 1945 or '46.
I don't know.
I have the book, but I don't know the date exactly.
I have the book here if you want to see later on.
Then I can-- I have the book with me.
We can look [INAUDIBLE] up the dates.
But it was-- I'm, I'm sure it was in 19-- in the beginning 1946 or the end of 1945.
Stanley, for those of us who were not there and who really don't understand the atmosphere in Poland, can you explain why, after the war, after so much suffering, this would have happened?
I don't know, but they hated the Jews is number one, and then the Jews came back, and they maybe want to claim their, their their, their real estate or whatever, their houses.
Property, property.
Property, their property.
Some of 'em were well to do, like the Lonek Lindenberger was one of the richest-- his father, one of-- one of the richest men in Nowy Targ.
And the Ludwig Hertz I mentioned was the son of a attorney, who was quite well-- but no, he was a-- he was a doctor I think.
I think it was-- it was-- it was attorney-- were quite well to do.
They owned large pieces of property in city of Nowy Targ.
And I guess they didn't want to see the Jews coming back and, and, and, and to live between, between them again and to claim the property.
I know that I came to my friends, to, to my neighbors, which I knew well.
They were mine age, was going to school.
So the whole-- the first thing that they ask me, are you still alive?
[POLISH]?
Are you still alive?
They couldn't believe that I survived.
They thought they got-- they, they, they, they, they wished, maybe, that they killed all the Jews out.
Well, that's-- you know, that's that's how you could interpret it, you know, maybe.
I don't know, there was-- [POLISH].
But I felt that [POLISH] that they, you know, that are you still alive, that they didn't kill you?
No sympathy whatsoever.
I was-- I know now I, I've come back again to the date which they shipped all the Jews out from Nowy Targ, which I found out from my neighbors the details.
My mother and the two-- and the brothers and the sisters, the date of, of that last day in Nowy Targ, which they supposed to come to a certain place to be, you know, shipped to the east.
They were-- they were-- they were going to, to the east, eastern, to Ukraine, to eastern Poland to work.
My mother and the two kids ran away from the house.
They, they were-- they, they ran into-- we hid behind a house.
We had like a little forest.
So they opened the back of the-- of the-- of the back yard.
And they ran, and they ran to some Polish people, by noontime I guess.
The Polish people wouldn't, wouldn't keep them.
I don't know what happened anyway.
They came back walking down the streets, my mother holding my brother and sister, walking down the streets [SOBBING] back-- back to the city, back to Nowy Targ.
Can you imagine a little kid holding-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, the whole transport was underway already.
So they took him [? out-- ?] they took him [? to ?] the cemetery and they shot him.
They shot him.
They shot him right in the cemetery.
That's what I know from the Polacks, what they tell me.
And that's where-- and that's where they, they are buried there in a-- in a grave, I guess.
I don't know where.
A unpicked mass grave, I guess.

But I can just [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] think about it, how they could-- how they-- how they felt coming down and taking them down and walking them [? down ?] to the cemetery-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the fright of the little kids, the fright of my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yes.
My sister was 10 years younger than me, and my brother was, was five year-- no, she was five years younger than me, and my brother was 10 years younger than me.
She was still in teenage years, going to school.
Pardon me?
That's what happened September.
That was 1942.
Oh, yeah [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] We have-- the city of Nowy Targ, we have all the people-- we have a [NON-ENGLISH], they call it, we remember 'em every year.
And when was it?
About, I think, three years ago, we went back to Poland.
People from Nowy Targ gather in Poland, people from the United States, still survivors, people from Israel, and some people from London came back to Nowy Targ.
And we-- and we-- and we go to the monument on the cemetery in the city of Nowy Targ at the big mass grave, which is there.
In the cemetery, right?
Right.
Well, me and my wife went back here from, from, from, from Los Angeles, [INAUDIBLE] because from Nowy Targ, there's not that many people left.
I have here one more-- one more friend living in-- in Los Angeles, which he is from Nowy Targ.
That's about it.
Friedman.
Is Saul Friedman.
Sam ?] Friedman.
Most of them-- most of them came from Israel.
I think from Israel we came maybe 30, 30 people in a bus load.
And one came from Miami, which is from Nowy Targ.
And one is living in, in London.
He came from London.
Yes.
Well, we get-- we got up in the morning and walked to the school.
We didn't live in the school.
We lived in three houses.
And they were-- they were put with barbed wire.
The guard-- the guards were all Ukrainians.
And we were marched every day to school-- to the school to work.
A lot of people worked.
Some of them worked in the-- feeding the cows or the pigs and cleaning the-- cleaning the streets, whatever.
I worked on building the, the target range.
And we built it for, for maybe six months.

So what was the, the whole-- the whole job was to, to work with a pic and to pick it up with shovels, put it on a-- on a wheelbarrow and take away the mud and haul it away and build a [? big-- ?] a [? big ?] target range, which was-- I don't know how many yards.
It must have been about maybe 200 or 300 yards long, you know.
And then we built the road coming to the-- to the-- to the target range and inside the target range.
And we built bunkers, you know.
When they-- when they-- when they built the target range, they set up targets.
There had to be a bunker, big, big bunkers that the people could move, move around in the back and set up the targets.
And that's how I worked over there.
It was very hard work.
Water coming down.
We had a lot of spring, spring water in the hills.
Water and mud and mud and water, and we had to pull that out and dig that out, and no machinery, no nothing.
Everything they did by hand.
And in September 1942, the same day when they shipped out all the Jews from, from Nowy Targ, one guy from Nowy Targ ran away from Rabka, just ran away.
He disappeared.
We couldn't find him.
So they took the eight people from Nowy Targ which we were there, and they put us in a, a-- they call it a bunker.
They put us in a-- in a-- in a cellar.
And they say we gonna be killed because of the guy who ran away from Nowy Targ, young people.
At least eight people are gonna be killed because he's from Nowy Targ.
Then a few hours later, they took us out from the cellar into the-- into the forest.
We had a forest there.
And we had to dig a grave.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they-- and we presumed that we gonna get killed here, you know.
And we had to dig a grave.
And they took us back in the-- in the-- in the late afternoon to the Appellplatz.
We had the Appellplatz which we gathered every morning and every evening.
They count us up, how many people showed up, and how many people-- nobody disappeared.
Every-- every day was counting, counting, counting.
They took us back on the-- on the-- on the counting on the Appellplatz.
They called up again the eight people.
We stay in the-- in the-- on the corner and we waited.
And all the guards-- the Gestapo came out, the Rosenbaum-- especially the Rosenbaum who ran the, the, the school.
And they took 10 Jews out from, from-- a little bit older Jews from Nowy Sacz.
And we had to watch him-- how they hanged them.
And they hanged 10 Jews.
And our job was to cut them down and to drag 'em to the forest and to bury 'em.
And one guy between those people was a guy who used to be a-- like a, a cleaner for the-- for the-- for the Rosenbaum.
He brought him back from Lemberg.
He was like his-- what they call that-- handyman around him, a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what-- what I mean.
And I don't know why he wanna hang this particularly guy too.
He wanna hang him between the 10 Jews.
And the guy-- they was thin, thin strings, and he was just, just falling, and they were-- what they call it?
They ripped the-- you know, they falling down.
They couldn't hold.
The, the strings were too thin.
And he begged them, [GERMAN].
Shoot me.
He wouldn't.
And they hanged him so many times, about three, four times.
That's what I was on a trial, and that's what we testified.
And we had to pull these other people-- most of them were still alive.
They were not hanged.
They were just [INAUDIBLE].
We used to carry them.
We were-- we were just happy that we were not them, because, you know, we were-- we were-- we were sure they were gonna kill us.
And we had to bury him.
[SIGHS] That was-- that was my incident in Rabka what I had.
And that's how I testified in, in Hamburg.
And not only me, all the people which came back to testify, which they-- we went through that particularly incident, that experience we went through.
From 200 people, like I told you, from 19-- [INAUDIBLE] 1942 till 1943, we, we were left with 66 people.
Out of 200, 66 survived.
Yep.
We came to Plaszow in 1942-- '43.
We were 66 people delivered to Plaszow.
Oh, yeah, oh yeah, which, which got-- which never survived, sure.
A lot of friends.
They were-- they call it Aktions, Aktions.
They call it Aktions.
Every so often, there's a graveyard in Rabka in the, the forest.
They built quite a few monuments.
Every few, few, few weeks or few months, they brought people in from, from the outside.
In other words, what kind of people?
People which were on, on Polish papers, Aryan.
They brought them into Rabka.
And they were-- they, you know, they were-- they were--
Interrogated.
Interrogated and beaten and something.
They kept them in the cellar.
And after a few days, we had to dig a grave.
And they, they were executed right there.
Right.
Oh, yeah.
False Aryan papers, yes.
And they were delivered, you know, by Polish police or by, by the Germans quite-- quite a few times, quite a-- quite a few graves.
And we had to be there-- we, we, we were-- they were at the graveyard for it.
Oh, yeah.
We were-- we were under command of-- we were digging graves and, and burying the people.
No, no.
No, no.
We were not commando.
We were-- we, we, we had a [GERMAN], in other words, a kapo, between all the people.
And that was his job.
Then he picked up the people and sent them.
And most of the time, we used to go.
It was his particularly job.
Yes.
Guards were strictly Ukrainians.
Oh, yeah, miserable.
Terrible, terrible.
They were not the guards-- they, they didn't watch us at work, but they would just march us to, to work from our-- from our quarters.
And they were guards at our quarters.
And they-- and they shot two guys right on the-- right at the quarters.
What happened, the way-- [?
We ?] [? knew ?] that they were paid off to-- for the-- from some people, which they lived in Rabka.
The Jews lived in Rabka, and they were with us in the camp.
We were from Nowy Targ.
They were from Rabka.
And they knew some Polacks outside of, of the-- of the camp to go and to organize something, to buy something.
And then we are-- we know that they, you know, they give him some kind of zlotys or something or gold or watches or whatever, to let him go out at night or something and to, to organize something.
And they killed two people.
The way-- the way we knew later on that they were paid off-- the guard was paid off by the, by the Jews.
And then he came back and he shot him anyway, outside of the-- of the-- of the barbed wire.
The name was a guy which, which was-- which was known for cruelty, was-- I forget his name.
Young, young Ukrainians, mostly all Ukrainians.
They were in uniforms, in SS uniforms.
They were just born anti-Semites, I guess.
They were-- they were trained by the Germans.
They were born-- they were Jew haters.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They could kick you, they could kick you.
They hit you with, with the-- with the thing they did.
And they were just guards.
They were guards just for the-- for the-- for the houses we were in.
That was-- that was their job.
They were not guarding us at work.
It was not their job.
They just guarding us, and we should not run away, I guess.
That's all.
Of course not.
From the guards, you mean?
Oh, yeah, you know.
I remember a, you know, a special incident, was a guy working named-- he was named Pikerny.
He used to work in the garage.
They used to have a truck and a-- and a car in the school.
And he used to work in the garage.
And one day-- it was on Sunday.
He used to work on Sundays too, you know.
On Sunday came out a guy, an SS name Oder.
He used to be [GERMAN] or something.
He came down, and I guess he was drunk or something.
And he took that guy out and he just told him to dig a grave and just shot him just right, right there, just, just for fun.
Oh, yeah.
Of course.
You lived from day to day.
We never thought that we gonna go out alive from Rabka.
And was no place to go.
Was no place to run.
Mm-hmm.
I wanna tell you before we change tapes, and we're doing the tapes--
